title: Enhancing the corrosion resistance of low-density steels via Al-rich oxide film formation by grain size optimization
abstract_summary: This study investigates how grain size, adjusted by solution treatment temperature (950–1150 °C), affects oxide film formation and corrosion resistance in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Fine-grained sample S950 (8.7 μm) rapidly forms a thick, Al-rich, defect-poor oxide film, yielding a low corrosion rate of 0.160 mm/y. Coarse-grained S1150 (32 μm) forms a thinner, less Al-rich, more defective film, increasing corrosion rate by 43%. The mechanism is linked to grain boundary density and preferential dissolution of Al at boundaries.
research_objective: To clarify the mechanism by which grain size controls the formation of Al-rich oxide films and the resulting corrosion resistance in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, addressing the knowledge gap beyond precipitate effects.
main_process_variables: Grain boundary density (HAGBs density 0.114 to 0.019 μm/μm²)
Corrosion test medium: 3.5 wt% NaCl solution

sample_aliases.meaning: Solution treated at 950 °C, average grain size 8.7 μm, fine-grained
Solution treated at 1050 °C, medium grain size
Solution treated at 1150 °C, average grain size 32 μm, coarse-grained

key_sections.description: Background on LDSs corrosion, role of Al and Mn, microalloying summary, and motivation for grain size optimization over compositional changes.
Material preparation, microstructural characterization, electrochemical measurements, immersion test, oxide film analysis.
Microstructure analysis (grain size, boundary density), electrochemical properties, oxide film analysis (XPS, thickness, defect density).
Effect of solution temperature on microstructure, thermodynamic analysis of oxide formation, and mechanism linking grain size/HAGBs to oxide film uniformity and corrosion resistance.
Summary of grain size effect on corrosion resistance, oxide film composition, and growth mechanism.
